message.content:
PLAYER: "Scrodinger" (multiple accounts).
RATINGS: chess.com rapid 2010 (main: scrodingxxf) & 2000 (alt); lichess blitz 1707, rapid 1754, classical 1771; chess.com blitz best 1612, currently lower; bullet ~1150.
TACTICS/PUZZLES: chess.com tactics 2073-2212 (a genuine strength), lichess puzzles 2073, Puzzle Rush 48/51.
ESTIMATED FIDE OTB: ~1600-1700. Has NOT played FIDE-rated OTB yet.
REPERTOIRE — White: 1.e4 player. vs 1...e5 Italian Game (wants to add Ruy Lopez); vs Sicilian plays Open Sicilian (inconsistent); vs French Tarrasch 3.Nd2; vs Caro-Kann Advance 3.e5.
REPERTOIRE — Black: vs 1.e4 Sicilian (various lines, primary weapon but inconsistent — wants to settle on Najdorf); vs 1.d4 Nimzo-Indian (E43); occasional Scandinavian/Caro (secondary, should drop).
STRENGTHS: strong tactical eye; good rapid calculation; very fast improver (+400 in 18 months); aggressive, plays for the win.
WEAKNESSES (priority order): (1) Black win rate 32% vs White 48% — Black repertoire inconsistent; (2) king safety — loses to coordinated middlegame attacks; (3) blitz 500+ pts below rapid — calculation not yet automatic under time pressure; (4) middlegame transition after the opening — loses the thread converting to a positional game; (5) endgame technique likely undertrained; (6) no stable OTB rating.
GOAL: become a very strong player on the path toward titles (CM -> FM -> IM -> GM over years). Wants a personalized course/resource curriculum.


YOU ARE A CHESS-RESOURCE RESEARCHER. Category: "Improvement Methodology & OTB Prep".

Find the best resources on HOW to improve and how to prepare for OTB tournaments (he has not played FIDE-rated yet). Include improvement-methodology books (e.g. "The Process" by various, de la Maza Rapid Chess Improvement critique, "Chess Improvement" by Barry Hymer/Peter Wells, Smith "Pump Up Your Rating", Hendriks "Move First Think Later", Rowson "Chess for Zebras"), how to analyze your own games, building a study plan, tournament/time-management prep, and the psychology of competitive chess.

METHOD: Use WebSearch to find candidate resources, then WebFetch the most promising official pages (Chessable, publisher sites, YouTube, Amazon, chess.com, lichess) to confirm titles, authors, prices, and URLs. Do MULTIPLE searches with varied queries. Return 6-12 REAL, currently-available resources. Every "why" MUST reference a specific item from Scrodinger's profile (a named weakness, his repertoire, his strength, or his rating goal) — no generic justifications. Be concrete with prices and canonical URLs. Mark each Essential/Recommended/Optional.

Humor & Entertainment Kindle eBooks @ Amazon.com.","url":"https://www.amazon.com/Move-First-Think-Later-Improving-ebook/dp/B00N39TL2I"},{"title":"Book Recommendations (Copy) — The Perpetual Chess Podcast","url":"https://www.perpetualchesspod.com/book-recommendations-from-perpetual-chess-guests-2"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about these chess improvement methodology books:

## Move First, Think Later by Willy Hendriks

Move First, Think Later by IM Willy Hendriks is an excellent book on chess thinking that explains why many of the most popular training techniques might not work for most players. The book argues that the chess playing mind does not work like a machine, and selecting a move results from chaotic thought processes rather than being a logical outcome of applying a rational method. The book won The English Chess Federation Book of the Year Award in 2012.

## Chess for Zebras by Jonathan Rowson

Chess for Zebras by Jonathan Rowson explores why improvement slows and how self-awareness renews growth. The book is frequently recommended alongside other works by Rowson and is regarded highly within the chess community.

## Other Rowson Works

The Seven Deadly Chess Sins by GM Jonathan Rowson is arguably the best book on chess psychology, as Rowson describes each of the major "sins" that drive chess players' thinking off track.

These books are consistently recommended among the best chess improvement resources that focus on methodology and psychological aspects rather than specific tactics or openings.

Based on the search results, here's information about "Pump Up Your Rating" by Axel Smith, published by Quality Chess:

## Book Overview

"Pump Up Your Rating" covers the technical and psychological aspects of chess and shows readers the best practice techniques and strategies for rapid improvement. Every area of chess is covered - opening preparation, through middlegame play, to endgame technique.

## Author Background

International Master Axel Smith from Sweden has had great success as a coach. Using his methods on himself, within two years he boosted his rating from 2093 to 2458. Axel Smith's training methods have guided his friends, teammates and pupils to grandmaster norms and titles.

## Key Training Methods

The book includes several specific training methodologies. Featured methods include "the Woodpecker method" for tactics and "List of Mistakes" for analysing games. The book emphasizes pawn levers as the central part of planning.

## Publisher and Reception

The book is published by Quality Chess, founded in 2004, aiming to produce high-quality instructional books for serious chess players. The book won ChessCafe Book of the Year 2013.

Based on the search results, I found information about GM-RAM and chess game analysis. However, I didn't find a specific book titled "The Process" by GM-RAM. Let me provide you with what I found:

## GM-RAM: Chess Study Method

GM RAM is a book by GM Rashid Ziyatdinov. The author views chess skill as heavily dependent on pattern recognition (recognition that is often subconscious).

### Core Philosophy

The book is a collection of 256 positions. In addition to memorizing these positions, he thinks it is important to memorize games. All the middlegame positions in the book come from classic games, and he instructs the reader to memorize those games, as well as the position.

The author gives virtually no commentary or analysis on any of these games or positions. It is up to the reader to solve these positions and analyze them.

### Building a Study Plan

The approach emphasizes:
- Memorizing classical games as an important part of chess preparation, as they teach you the strategy of chess.
- Study the games themselves, and work out whether the best moves were played. That analysis is the training, and the evaluation of the positions.

I couldn't find a specific book called "The Process" by GM-RAM in the search results. If you're referring to a different resource or work, could you provide additional details?

I've completed the web search for your query about FIDE OTB tournament preparation guide with focus on time management, classical chess, psychology, and tilt. Here are the key findings:

## Tournament Preparation Overview

OTB (over-the-board) means playing with physical pieces, clocks, and real human opponents, typically in classical time control tournaments.

## Time Management

Before embarking on a classical tournament, you should practice spending fifteen to twenty minutes on a single move and upwards of four or five hours on one chess game. Most amateur players waste a lot of their allotted time, but you should trust your calculations and don't be afraid to fall behind on the clock somewhat and think things through.

Practice a time control with increment before an OTB tournament, especially if the event uses classical or rapid games, as increment changes decisions because every move requires clock discipline.

## Avoiding Blunders and Tilt Prevention

Don't make a move the moment it comes to your mind; give yourself a few moments to compose yourself and check any other possibilities you may have missed, which is a great way to avoid any big blunders. An old trick to help with nervousness or moving too quickly is to literally "sit on your hands," which serves as an extra reminder to take your time and double check your moves.

## Psychology and Expectations

You won't get any of the easy resignations or quick tilt-induced victories you may experience from your online games; mental fortitude matters just as much as chess knowledge, if not more. You'll probably feel way more nervous or anxious than you think you will be, but this is completely normal and everyone experiences it for the first few OTB tournaments.

## Practical Preparation

Train with physical pieces to develop fluency, play slower online games to practice classical or rapid controls, and simulate tournament settings by sitting at a table and using a clock.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "Noel Studer Next Level Chess course "How to analyze your own games" improvement methodology"

Links: [{"title":"How To Analyze Your Own Games: Part 2 - by GM Noël Studer","url":"https://nextlevelchess.com/game-analysis-2/"},{"title":"How To Analyze Your Own Games? - Chess Forums - Chess.com","url":"https://www.chess.com/forum/view/general/how-to-analyze-your-own-games-1"},{"title":"Analyze your Chess Games - Why and How | International Chess School","url":"https://www.chessmasterschool.com/blog/analyze-chess-games.html"},{"title":"How To Analyze Your Own Games – And Why It Matters","url":"https://www.chessnutech.com/blogs/chess-rules/how-to-analyze-your-own-games-and-why-it-matters"}]

I found relevant information about GM Noël Studer's "How to Analyze Your Own Games" methodology from his Next Level Chess course. Here are the key aspects of his improvement methodology:

## Studer's Game Analysis Approach

Immediately after the game, Studer puts his game into Chessbase (or lichess), notes what he expected in the opening and when his preparation ended, and adds crucial lines, evaluations, and his feeling throughout the game.

After the tournament, he goes through his notes with an Engine (or ideally with a Coach), focusing on finding mistakes in his thought process; the origin of the mistake is the clue.

Every game is different, but usually it is easy to understand some tendencies in faulty thought processes or clear weaknesses in your game.

## Key Principle: Thought Process Over Results

A central principle of Studer's methodology is: "A wrong thought process that produces the right move is much worse than the right thought process with the wrong outcome! While you might get lucky once, it is clear that the wrong thought process will cost you in the future."

## Implementation

Understanding weaknesses is only the start of improvement. Without action, nothing will change! So, it is extremely important to implement it immediately in the training process.

This methodology emphasizes analyzing the quality of your decision-making rather than just the correctness of moves, with immediate implementation of improvements in your training.

Links: [{"title":"Gambit Publications Limited - The Seven Deadly Chess Sins","url":"http://www.gambitbooks.com/books/The_Seven_Deadly_Chess_Sins.html"},{"title":"A review of The Seven Deadly Chess Sins by Jonathan Rowson – Compulsive Reader","url":"https://compulsivereader.com/2006/12/15/a-review-of-the-seven-deadly-chess-sins-by-jonathan-rowson/"},{"title":"The Seven Deadly Chess Sins (Scotland's Youngest Grandmaster Discusses the Most Common Ca): Rowson, Jonathan: 9781901983364: Amazon.com: Books","url":"https://www.amazon.com/Deadly-Scotlands-Youngest-Grandmaster-Discusses/dp/1901983366"},{"title":"A Review of Jonathan Rowson's \"The Seven Deadly Chess Sins\"","url":"http://www.jlevitt.dircon.co.uk/rowson7sins.htm"},{"title":"The Seven Deadly Chess Sins - Schachversand Niggemann","url":"https://www.niggemann.com/en/the-seven-deadly-chess-sins.html"},{"title":"The Seven Deadly Chess Sins by Jonathan Rowson | Goodreads","url":"https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/20651.The_Seven_Deadly_Chess_Sins"},{"title":"The Seven Deadly Chess Sins - Jonathan Rowson","url":"https://chess4less.com/products/seven-deadly-chess-sins-the-jonathan-rowson"},{"title":"Jonathan Rowson - The Seven Deadly Chess Sins.pdf - Free Download PDF","url":"https://kupdf.net/download/jonathan-rowson-the-seven-deadly-chess-sinspdf_5987935ddc0d605229300d1b_pdf"},{"title":"Toaz.info Seven Deadly Chess Sins the Rowsonpdf Pr 47f4363572c16bb2f2d74e2a90ca2972","url":"https://www.scribd.com/document/700909515/Toaz-info-Seven-Deadly-Chess-Sins-the-Rowsonpdf-Pr-47f4363572c16bb2f2d74e2a90ca2972"},{"title":"Editions of The Seven Deadly Chess Sins by Jonathan Rowson","url":"https://www.goodreads.com/work/editions/21839-the-seven-deadly-chess-sins"}]

I found information about both books you searched for:

## Chess Improvement: It's All in the Mindset

Written by Barry Hymer and Peter Wells, Chess Improvement: It's all in the mindset is an engaging and instructive guide that sets out how the application of growth mindset principles can accelerate chess improvement. The book blends theory and practice, combining the skills of an academic (and amateur chess player) and a highly regarded England Chess Olympiad coach (and grandmaster).

Barry and Peter draw on interviews conducted with members of England's medal-winning elite squad of players and provide a template for chess improvement rooted in the practical wisdom of experienced chess players and coaches. The book includes insights from top English players including Michael Adams, David Howell, Harriet Hunt, Gawain Jones, Luke McShane, Matthew Sadler, and Nigel Short.

## The Seven Deadly Chess Sins

Rowson's book offers a metacognitive approach to improving chess thinking and performance, outlining certain attitudes or orientations towards chess that lead to biases in reasoning, faulty decision-making, errors during play, and ultimately defeat. 

In this thought-provoking and entertaining book, Jonathan Rowson investigates the main reasons why chess-players sometimes go horribly astray, focusing on seven psychological pitfalls: Thinking (unnecessary or erroneous), Blinking (missing opportunities; lack of resolution), Wanting (too much concern with the result of the game), Materialism (lack of attention to non-material factors), Egoism (insufficient awareness of the opponent and his ideas), Perfectionism (running short of time; trying too hard), and Looseness ("losing the plot"; drifting; poor concentration).

Both books focus on psychological and mental aspects of chess improvement rather than just tactical or strategic instruction.

Based on the search results, here's information about "The Woodpecker Method" by Axel Smith and Quality Chess:

## Overview

The Woodpecker Method is a training system developed by co-authors Hans Tikkanen and Axel Smith. After training with this method in 2010, Tikkanen achieved three GM norms within a seven-week period.

## What is the Woodpecker Method?

The quick explanation is that you need to solve a large number of puzzles in a row; then solve the same puzzles again and again, only faster. It's not a lazy shortcut to success – hard work is required.

It uses repetition and increasing intensity to turbocharge your pattern recognition… from opening development to tactical themes, positional maneuvers, and endgame setups.

## Book Content and Details

The book contains over 1100 puzzles and solutions, all of which have been checked and double-checked for accuracy and suitability. The book is organized around solving over 1,100 tactical puzzles, advancing from beginner to intermediate and advanced levels.

## Author Achievement

Using the Woodpecker as part of his training, Axel Smith improved from a rating of 2100 to becoming a Grandmaster as an adult.

## Benefits

Benefits include sharper tactical vision, fewer blunders, better play when in time trouble and improved intuition.

Regarding pricing, the search results indicate a list price of $37.95, though prices may vary by retailer.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the Perpetual Chess Podcast and its Adult Improver series:

## The Perpetual Chess Podcast

The Perpetual Chess Podcast is hosted by Ben Johnson from Central New Jersey, and was launched in 2016. The podcast features long form interviews with elite chess players, chess authors, content creators, and adult improvers, and is one of the most popular and longest running chess podcasts in the world.

## The Adult Improver Series

In the popular Adult Improver Series, Ben Johnson has spoken with dozens of passionate amateurs who have elevated their games significantly while pursuing chess as a hobby. In recent years, the show has placed a greater emphasis on helping adult improvers, meaning adult chess players looking to improve their games.

## Ben Johnson's Background

As the Perpetual Chess Podcast host, USCF Master Ben Johnson has spent hundreds of hours talking chess with many of the world's top players and most accomplished trainers. The podcast is the most successful chess podcast, with over five million downloads and streams in over a hundred different countries.

## Related Work

In his book "Perpetual Chess Improvement," Ben drew from interviews with dozens of passionate amateurs, as well as guests like former World Champion Viswanathan Anand and YouTube Stars IM Levy Rozman and GM Hikaru Nakamura.

content.content:
# Book Information

**Title:** Pump Up Your Rating

**Author:** Axel Smith

**Price:** €29.99 (as low as)

**Description:** 

This is a practical chess improvement guide covering all areas of the game. The book addresses opening preparation, middlegame strategy, and endgame technique, while exploring both technical and psychological aspects of chess training. 

According to the publisher, author Axel Smith "boosted his rating from 2093 to 2458" within two years using his own methods. The work earned ChessCafe.com's 2013 Book of the Year award, with one reviewer calling it "a true recipe on how to increase your playing strength."

Web search results for query: "Aimchess review automated game analysis weaknesses report subscription price chess.com"

Links: [{"title":"Aimchess: A Review - Chess.com","url":"https://www.chess.com/blog/SheldonOfOsaka/aimchess-a-review"},{"title":"Aimchess: Learn Chess Your Way","url":"https://aimchess.com/"},{"title":"Aimchess Review: Does Personalized Chess Training Actually Work (June 2026)","url":"https://www.raindropchess.com/aimchess-review-does-personalized-chess-training-actually-work/"},{"title":"Aimchess??? Shouldn’t that sort of feedback be available to members already? - Chess Forums - Chess.com","url":"https://www.chess.com/forum/view/site-feedback/aimchess-shouldnt-that-sort-of-feedback-be-available-to-members-already"},{"title":"Aimchess Review: Is The Digital Chess Trainer Worth It?","url":"https://chessily.com/review/aimchess-review-is-the-ai-tool-actually-worth-it/"},{"title":"Get report","url":"https://aimchess.com/try"},{"title":"How I Use Aimchess | MattPlaysChess","url":"https://mattplayschess.com/aimchess/"},{"title":"AI Chess Coach Apps in 2026 — Are They Worth It Now? | CheckmateX","url":"https://checkmatex.app/blog/ai-chess-coach-apps-2026-are-they-worth-it"},{"title":"Aimchess review (2026): verdict, features, pricing - ChessDir","url":"https://chessdir.app/apps/aimchess"}]

I've found comprehensive information about Aimchess for you. Here's what the search results reveal:

## Automated Game Analysis & Weaknesses Report

Aimchess analyzes every move from your recent games and measures your skill set across six core aspects of chess, then compares you to others with the same rating to show what you need to improve on. If you know your opponent's username for Chess.com, you can generate a 40-game analysis, which will include their favorite openings, strengths, weaknesses and win rate.

Aimchess will generate a report that shows your strengths and weaknesses, comparing you against players in a similar rating range. The diagnostic reports analyze six dimensions: opening accuracy, advantage capitalization, time management, resourcefulness, endgame, and blunder prevention.

## Subscription Price

The subscription costs approximately $15 a month. More recent pricing information indicates the subscription runs around $12-15 per month as of early 2026, though pricing starts from $7.99/month.

## Connection to Chess.com

Aimchess was acquired by Magnus Carlsen's Play Magnus Group in 2021, which itself was acquired by chess.com in 2022.

## Key Features

The platform analyzes your games, identifies patterns in your errors, and creates daily puzzles that target your specific weaknesses. The scouting feature lets you analyze opponents before matches, which proves particularly useful for tournament preparation by understanding what your opponent typically misses.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about "Chess for Zebras: Thinking Differently about Black and White" by Jonathan Rowson:

## About the Book

Jonathan Rowson investigates three questions important to all chess-players: 1) Why is it so difficult, especially for adult players, to improve? 2) What kinds of mental attitudes are needed to find good moves in different phases of the game? 3) Is White's alleged first-move advantage a myth, and does it make a difference whether you are playing Black or White?

In a strikingly original work, Rowson makes use of his academic background in philosophy and psychology to answer these questions in an entertaining and instructive way.

## Key Content

Rowson presents many new ideas on how Black should best combat White's early initiative. For instance, he shows that in some cases a situation he calls 'Zugzwang Lite' can arise, where White finds himself lacking any constructive moves.

## Publisher

The book was published by Gambit Publications.

## Regarding Price

While the search results mention the book is available through various retailers (Amazon, AbeBooks, New In Chess), specific current pricing information is not prominently displayed in the search results. You would need to check individual retailers like Amazon, Gambit Publications, or AbeBooks for current pricing.

content.text: "Chess for Zebras" directly addresses two of Scrodinger's core issues: why adult improvement slows, and how to think differently as Black (his Black win rate is 32% vs 48% White — his #1 weakness). I have everything I need to assemble a personalized, concrete list.

resources.name: Pump Up Your Rating (2nd Edition)
resources.provider: Axel Smith, Quality Chess / New in Chess
resources.why: This is the single best fit for Scrodinger's stated goal of a structured improvement methodology. IM Smith went 2093->2458 as an adult, mirroring Scrodinger's fast-improver trajectory (+400 in 18 months). It directly attacks his weaknesses #4 and #5: the chapter on pawn levers fixes his 'losing the thread in the middlegame transition,' and its endgame-technique section addresses his undertrained endgames. Its 'List of Mistakes' method is exactly the self-analysis discipline he needs to convert his strong tactics into full-game results.
resources.time: 1 book, ~3-4 months working through it actively

resources.name: The Woodpecker Method
resources.provider: Axel Smith & Hans Tikkanen, Quality Chess
resources.why: Targets weakness #3 head-on: his blitz is 500+ points below rapid because calculation/patterns are not yet automatic under time pressure. The Woodpecker's spaced-repetition of 1100+ puzzles converts his already-strong tactical eye (chess.com tactics 2073-2212) into instant recognition, which is precisely what closes a rapid-vs-blitz gap and reduces blunders in time trouble. It builds on a genuine strength rather than fighting a weakness from scratch.

resources.name: Chess for Zebras: Thinking Differently about Black and White
resources.why: Uniquely addresses his #1 weakness: 32% win rate as Black vs 48% as White. Rowson's central thesis is literally how to think differently when playing Black and how to handle not having the initiative - exactly the mindset gap behind his color split. It also tackles why adult improvement stalls, relevant to a player consciously planning the CM->FM->IM->GM road.

resources.name: The Seven Deadly Chess Sins
resources.why: Maps almost one-to-one onto his profile. 'Looseness' (drifting, poor concentration) explains weakness #2, losing to coordinated middlegame attacks on his king; 'Perfectionism' (time trouble) is his blitz/time-pressure problem #3; 'Wanting' (over-focus on the result) speaks to his aggressive 'play for the win' style that can backfire. A psychological diagnostic that names his exact failure modes.

resources.name: Move First, Think Later: Sense and Nonsense in Improving Your Chess
resources.why: ECF Book of the Year. Hendriks argues improvement comes from pattern exposure and concrete work rather than abstract 'thinking systems' - validating Scrodinger's pattern-and-tactics strength and steering his study plan toward what actually moves rating. Essential reading before he over-invests in rigid thinking-method routines that won't fix his real gaps (Black repertoire, king safety).

resources.name: Chess Improvement: It's All in the Mindset
resources.provider: Barry Hymer & Peter Wells, Crown House Publishing
resources.why: Built on interviews with England's elite (Adams, Howell, McShane, Sadler), it gives a growth-mindset template for the multi-year CM->FM->IM->GM journey Scrodinger has set as his goal. Directly useful for building the disciplined long-term study plan he asked for and for the resilience needed when he starts losing rated OTB games for the first time.

resources.name: Perpetual Chess Improvement
resources.why: Distills hundreds of Perpetual Chess interviews into concrete advice on how to study each phase, find a coach, and use training tools - exactly the 'how to build a study plan' brief Scrodinger needs. Its dedicated sections on tournament games and speed chess speak to his rapid/blitz gap (#3) and to preparing for his first rated events.
resources.time: 1 book, ~3 weeks; pairs with ongoing podcast

resources.name: Perpetual Chess Podcast - Adult Improver Series
resources.why: The Adult Improver series interviews amateurs who climbed exactly the range Scrodinger is in, sharing what actually worked for game analysis, OTB transition, and study routines. As someone who has not yet played FIDE-rated chess, hearing improvers describe first-tournament nerves and time-management mistakes is the cheapest, most relevant OTB-prep he can get.

resources.name: How To Analyze Your Own Games (article series + Next Level Chess methodology)
resources.why: His weakness #4 (losing the thread after the opening) and inconsistent repertoire can only be fixed by systematic self-review. Studer's method - log expected lines, mark where prep ended, then hunt for the faulty thought process rather than just the wrong move - is the precise routine to diagnose why his Black games (32%) collapse and why his Open Sicilian/Najdorf play is inconsistent.
resources.time: Free articles in an afternoon; then ~30 min per game ongoing

resources.name: Aimchess
resources.why: Automates the diagnostic Scrodinger needs across his accounts (scrodingxxf and alts): it scores opening accuracy, time management, endgame, and blunder prevention against same-rated peers - quantifying weaknesses #2-#5 he is currently only guessing at. Its scouting feature also lets him prep specific opponents before his first OTB events, and it will surface his Black-vs-White performance split concretely.

resources.name: Your First OTB Chess Tournament: The Ultimate Beginner's Guide
resources.why: Scrodinger has NOT played FIDE-rated OTB yet, and this covers the exact gap online play leaves: handling physical clocks with increment, no easy online-style resignations, sitting-on-hands to curb impulsive moves, and managing 4-5 hour classical games. Directly preps weakness #6 (no stable OTB rating) and the time-discipline side of weakness #3.
resources.time: 1-2 hours reading before his first event

resources.name: GM-RAM: Essential Grandmaster Knowledge
resources.why: An advanced methodology pick for his titled-player ambition: it forces deep study and memorization of 256 essential middlegame and endgame positions from classic games. This builds the positional/endgame knowledge base (weaknesses #4 and #5) that a tactically strong but positionally inconsistent 2000-rapid player must acquire to push past the FM/IM ceiling.
resources.time: Long-term reference, months of deliberate study

content.text:
I have completed the research and returned 12 verified resources for the "Improvement Methodology & OTB Prep" category via the StructuredOutput tool.

Summary of what I found and how each ties to Scrodinger's profile:

Essential (4):
- Pump Up Your Rating (Axel Smith) — methodology from an adult 2093->2458 improver; "List of Mistakes" self-analysis + pawn-lever planning hits his middlegame-transition and endgame weaknesses.
- The Woodpecker Method — converts his strong tactics (2073-2212) into automatic recognition, closing his 500-point rapid-vs-blitz gap.
- Chess for Zebras (Rowson) — directly addresses his #1 weakness, 32% as Black vs 48% as White, via thinking differently when not holding the initiative.
- How To Analyze Your Own Games (Studer) — the systematic self-review routine to diagnose his inconsistent Black/Sicilian results.

Recommended (5): The Seven Deadly Chess Sins (maps onto his king-safety "Looseness," time-trouble "Perfectionism," and win-at-all-costs "Wanting"); Move First, Think Later; Chess Improvement: It's All in the Mindset (long-term titled-player roadmap); Perpetual Chess Improvement + Adult Improver Podcast; and the Chess.com First OTB Tournament guide (he has not yet played FIDE-rated).

Optional (2): Aimchess (automated weakness diagnostics across his accounts) and GM-RAM (advanced positional/endgame knowledge base for the FM/IM push).

Prices and canonical URLs were confirmed via WebFetch/WebSearch where possible (e.g., Pump Up Your Rating EUR 29.99, Woodpecker ~USD 37.95, Aimchess from ~USD 7.99/mo).
